pairs.archetype_description: Four-axis anti-AI: GPT-impossible opener, obscure company reference, non-formula structure, front-loaded typo.
pairs.what_to_notice: Which email feels like it was written by a real person? Which one would you actually reply to?

version_a.subject_line: a question about yale new haven's fiscal 2025 losses
version_a.email_body:
Hi Jennifer,

Saw reports that Yale New Haven Health closed fiscal 2025 down nearly $200 million, with Medicaid shortfalls over $430M and a new CFO just stepping in this March. Strategic operations at a five-hospital system carrying that kind of pressure tends to mean every dollar of coding accuracy matters more than it did 12 months ago.

One Director at a similar academic system cut denial rates 27% and recovered cash flow without adding to the billing team.

Directors use autonomous coding to bring coding costs down 40% while holding accuracy above 98%. Curious whether that math is worth a closer look for Yale New Haven's setup?

version_b.email_body:
dont know if this lands Jennifer, but the Wallingford urgent care opening post you put up caught me at a weird time.

Five hospitals, a $200M loss year, Medicaid shortfall north of $430M, and you're standing up new outpatient footprint anyway. That's a strategic ops job most people wouldn't want.

The coding side of a build-while-bleeding year is where the quiet money hides. One similar system found 27% of their denials were coming from the newest sites, not the ones they assumed.

Anyway. If Arnim's first 90 days include a look at coding accuracy, I'd love to be in that conversation.

pairs.archetype_description: 3-4 lines, ~50 words. Signal-led opener, what we do, the ask. LinkedIn-DM-short.
pairs.what_to_notice: Would the crisp 3-4 line version get a reply where the longer one gets ignored? Or does it feel too thin?

version_a.subject_line: a question about bristol hospice's de novo expansion
version_a.email_body:
Hi Rachael,

Noticed Bristol Hospice just launched de novos across four states in January, with five more markets named for the next wave. Standing up new operations that fast usually means coding teams are the last group to scale, and clean claims from day one across Mississippi, Tulsa, and McAllen is its own problem.

One VP at a similar multi-state hospice network cut A/R days by 30% during their expansion quarter without adding coders.

VPs use autonomous coding to accelerate cash flow and keep accuracy above 98% as new locations come online. Worth 15 minutes to see if that approach maps to Bristol's launch cadence?

version_b.email_body:
Hi Rachael, four de novo states in January plus five more markets coming is a lot of coding to spin up clean.

We help hospices keep accuracy above 98% across new sites while cutting A/R days 30%.

Open to a 15 minute chat?

pairs.archetype_description: Opens an open loop, hints at a pattern, does not resolve. CTA invites the payoff.
pairs.what_to_notice: Which version makes you want to keep reading?

version_a.subject_line: a question about agrace's wauwatosa expansion
version_a.email_body:
Hi Carrie,

Saw Agrace is extending into Ozaukee, Washington, Racine, and Kenosha out of the new Wauwatosa regional office while the $30M Dementia Village campaign keeps building. Regional operations stretched across new counties plus a capital project usually means the billing team is getting pulled thinner than anyone's naming out loud.

One Director at a similar hospice cut coding costs 40% during a regional expansion without growing their billing team.

Directors use autonomous coding to keep accuracy above 98% while operations scale. Worth a 15 minute look at how that would apply to Agrace's southeast Wisconsin rollout?

version_b.subject_line: something the wauwatosa expansion raised for us
version_b.email_body:
Hi Carrie,

Something about Agrace's move into Ozaukee, Washington, Racine, and Kenosha kept nagging at me, and it's not the obvious staffing question.

Every nonprofit hospice we've watched stretch into new counties hits the same invisible margin leak in month three. It isn't competition from for-profits, and it isn't recruitment. It's buried somewhere else entirely, and Dementia Village breaking ground in May probably accelerates it.

I'd rather not lay out the whole thing in a cold email, but it's one of those patterns that's easy to spot once you know where to look.

Would it be useful to walk through what we've seen, before the southeastern rollout picks up pace?

pairs.archetype_description: Anti-AI opener with an obscure reference and front-loaded typo vs a 3-line signal-led DM-short email. Which feels more human?
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
congrats Jessica, the associate's post popped up on my feed between two very forgettable things.

doing revenue integrity while also finishing a degree, with Nashville General sitting on an internal audit finding and a brand new FY26 board chair, is a lot of plates.

most places we talk to in that spot are a quarter away from needing a second set of coder eyes they cannot actually hire.

if FY26 ends up being a year you rebuild the coding workflow, I would love to be useful.

version_b.email_body:
Jessica, FY26 opening with a new board chair and an audit flag on financials is a tight window for revenue integrity.

We help hospitals cut denial rates 27% without adding coders.

Worth 15 minutes before the FY26 plan locks?

pairs.archetype_description: Anti-AI typed-fast human vs the current THE ONE framework. Which voice earns the reply?
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
congrats on the Women's Choice orthopedics nod Joe, genuinely didnt expect a Bowling Green hospital to show up next to the usual suspects.

VPs I talk to at community hospitals carrying that kind of recognition usually have a quieter story on the rev cycle side, where the same volume is moving through thinner coder teams than anyone wants to admit.

one VP at a similar size community hospital found their A/R days dropped 30% after pulling coding off the critical path.

no ask, just figured the contrast was worth naming.

version_b.subject_line: a question about wood county's outpatient recognition
version_b.email_body:
Hi Joe,

Saw Wood County Hospital picked up Women's Choice recognition for orthopedics and outpatient experience in 2026. Community hospitals running rev cycle and physician admin under one roof usually absorb that volume through the coding team, and that is where most of the margin quietly leaks.

One VP at a similar community hospital cut A/R days 30% in a quarter without adding coders.

VPs use autonomous coding to keep accuracy above 98% while cost to collect comes down 70%. Worth 15 minutes to see if Wood County's numbers line up?

pairs.archetype_description: 3-line signal-crisp DM vs an anonymized peer story. Brevity or narrative?
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
Melanie, the Castle Connolly #1 plus A-10 partnership year usually doubles coding volume before hiring can catch up.

We help ortho groups cut coding costs 40% while holding accuracy above 98%.

Open to 15 minutes?

version_b.subject_line: what a peer ortho group did post incident
version_b.email_body:
Hi Melanie,

A Director of Rev Cycle at a similar size ortho group was 18 months out from their own ransomware event and still running the stitched together coding workflow they set up in week one.

They assumed the workflow was fine because the queues were moving. Once they audited it, coding costs were 40% higher than peer benchmarks and accuracy was sitting just under 95%. After switching to autonomous coding, costs came down 40%, accuracy went to 98% plus, and the team got back the hours they had been spending on QA patches.

OrthoVirginia's post Ryuk setup sounds like the same starting point.

Worth 15 minutes?

pairs.archetype_description: Anti-AI human vs signal-crisp, second signal pairing for variance.
pairs.what_to_notice: Which opening signal hooks you more? Describe in one word what caught your eye in the winning version.

version_a.email_body:
dont know if the S.E.E. Project funding loss is the one that hurts most Joanne, or if its one of the other three.

515 employees, a $50m January 2027 target, and finance running cover on all of it means the coding and billing side of the house is probably the last thing getting attention and the first thing leaking.

one VP at a similar sized nonprofit network found that 30% of their A/R days were sitting in coder reviewable claims nobody had cycles for.

if that is a useful conversation ahead of FY26, I am around.

version_b.email_body:
Joanne, California's $90m backfill buys time, not a new cost structure.

We help networks cut coding costs 40% while holding accuracy above 98%.

Worth 15 minutes?
